Name: latency-aware_spectral_routing
Title: Latency-Aware Spectral Routing: Training-Free Inference Optimization via Activation Covariance Geometry
Short Hypothesis: The spectral properties of hidden state activations can serve as a direct proxy for computational difficulty, enabling dynamic, latency-aware routing of tokens through fewer layers or lower-precision computations without compromising accuracy.
Related Work: Recent works like Spectral Quantization Routing (SQR) and Spectral Latent Steering (SLS) have explored spectral properties for mixed-precision inference and test-time optimization. However, these approaches either focus on precision allocation or subspace projection, but do not combine both into a unified framework for latency-aware routing. Our proposal extends these ideas by introducing a dynamic routing mechanism that uses spectral diagnostics to enforce strict latency budgets while maintaining accuracy.
Abstract: Modern neural networks often face stringent latency constraints in deployment, especially in resource-constrained environments. Existing methods for latency-aware inference rely on heuristic pruning or static profiling, which fail to adapt to dynamic input distributions. We propose Latency-Aware Spectral Routing (LASR), a training-free framework that dynamically routes tokens through fewer layers or lower-precision computations based on the spectral properties of hidden state activations. Specifically, we compute the local covariance condition number and spectral decay rate of hidden states on-the-fly using randomized SVD, mapping these geometric diagnostics to a precision-switching and layer-skipping policy. We theoretically ground LASR with PAC-Bayes bounds linking spectral conditioning to generalization stability under quantization and layer-skipping noise. Evaluated on single-GPU setups across WILDS benchmarks, LASR achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to static and heuristic latency-aware baselines. By replacing qualitative fragility claims with formalized risk bounds, LASR provides a transparent, reproducible pathway for resource-efficient inference under natural distribution shifts.

Experiments.Description: Evaluate LASR on WILDS benchmarks (e.g., Camelyon17, iWildCam) with varying latency budgets.
Compare LASR against static mixed-precision and layer-skipping baselines.
Ablation study on the impact of spectral diagnostics (condition number, spectral decay rate) on routing decisions.
Measure the computational overhead of randomized SVD and its impact on inference latency.

Risk Factors and Limitations: The effectiveness of LASR may degrade under extreme input distribution shifts not covered in the training data.
The computational overhead of randomized SVD could outweigh the benefits of dynamic routing in some scenarios.
LASR relies on the assumption that spectral properties are consistent across different layers, which may not hold for all architectures.
